speaker: Rob Moore
words: Billy Kauder was born in 1868 in Illinois and at the age of 21 moved with his family to the Cedar Creek country where he and his father both took up homesteads. On this tape Billy talks about the Farmers Alliance, the homestead years, clearing land, the 1893 depression, the 1896 election, and early towns.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well you were here during the hard times around 1893 then weren't you?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I sure was.

speaker: William Kauder
words: That was some picking salt of it.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, right at that time I was 21. 21, 2 and 3.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well I should be in the making something and you couldn't get a job and you... it was some living.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did you do to get through those years?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well I'm getting a debt yet. I don't know how we managed that. It was slim picking out of it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you stay on the homestead?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yeah, on father's home still.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: At that time were there many people around here who were interested in that Coxey's army that was kind of protesting against the poor times?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, there was more people in here. There was quite a lot of homesteaders hadn't proved up yet, you know. In there where we were in there what is called Crescent now and London and beyond and there was all the homesteaders. They all lived on their places. They proved up and happened to, I think about 90's, 1890. About 1894 or 95 a lot of them proved up and mortgages and left the homesteads.

speaker: William Kauder
words: There was at that time more people in there that was more heavily populated than it is today.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well were they all mostly on small farms? Hmm? Were they mostly on small farms?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh no, they had 160 but they had to clear up if they wanted any cultivating land.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it pretty hard to clear that land up?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh gosh yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How would you go about it?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well we cut down a tree, trim him up and burn the brush, saw him up in about 8, 10, 12 feet lengths, take a team and break it up into log piles and set him on fire. There was thousands of dollars worth of good timber burns up just that way.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it too hard to get them to a mill?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Was no saw mill around then.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was the farmers alliance, or the farmers union working around here at that time?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, but I heard of around through the farming country.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kinds of things did they do?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How was that? What kinds of things did they do?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The farmers alliance?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes. I don't know. They started in, I guess meant pretty good. They were quite well organized yet they had the stores of their own that is grocery stores and hardware.

speaker: William Kauder
words: What the farmers needed. Uh huh.

speaker: William Kauder
words: But that didn't last long.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well what, were most people pretty angry about the hard times then?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh yes they were dissatisfied, they couldn't do nothing about it you know. But they got plenty of that free trade. You know,

speaker: William Kauder
words: Cleveland run on the Democratic ticket on a free trade. He believed that this country should, and other countries should exchange free you know. They shipped things in here free of duty and the American shipped out free of duty. That's the way they believed. And Harrison was the opposing nominee on the Republican ticket and he believed in the protective tariff. And that's where I picked my ticket. I consulted a lot of well educated men you know that had been through the bills and they told me that this free trade business wouldn't work. We had to have protective tariff. And I come to that conclusion myself that that was right because in them foreign countries, you take Germany, France, Russia, they had cheap labor. So much cheaper than we had here. And they manufactured their goods for less than half what we would hear. They get shipped again, you take the dry goods such as clothing. You could buy a shirt there for fifty cents. They couldn't make it in the United States for less than seventy -five for a dollar at that same time. It was just that they put everything, hardware, coffee, you could buy coffee then a bit a pound, eight pounds for a dollar. It was parts but not ground. There's packages, pound packages. There was two brands of it. There was the Lion's brand and Arbuckle.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well do you think it was it was Cleveland's free trade policies that brought on that 1893 depression?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I didn't exactly catch it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think it was Cleveland's free trade policy that brought on that 1893 depression?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Why of course.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Nothing else could bring it on.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well there was a bad harvest here that year too wasn't there?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No the harvest was all right but it's a loss quite a bit of grain on account of rain. It started about the middle of September and by gosh it did rain. I worked down in the Dickson country. I was with a farming machine down there. But we happened to get most of the crops taken care of before the rains come. They didn't come until about the middle of September.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was the next election after that? Who ran in the next election after that?

speaker: William Kauder
words: About four years later William Jennings Bryan ran again with William McKinley. Bryan's run on a free silver ticket, 16 silver dollars to one gold dollar. And McKinley run on the gold standard and McKinley won.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well didn't most of the working -class people support Bryan?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know about that. McKinley was elected.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I think the people, well I know around here there was an awful lot of people voted for McKinley regardless of whether the Democrats or Republicans voted for McKinley. They got that silver trade to the free trade to figure that out that way.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What were the early homesteaders like? What kind of people were they?

speaker: William Kauder
words: The best people in the world. There were people from Kansas, Oklahoma, Nebraska, Iowa, Missouri, Illinois, Ohio. I didn't know a thing about my neighbor. My neighbor didn't know a thing about me, whether I was a respectable character or not. But we never asked any questions. And we were neighbors.

speaker: William Kauder
words: They were all friendly, one with another.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did most people come out here?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well I have an idea.

speaker: William Kauder
words: But they didn't do so well in the East.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And there were a lot of homestead land at that time. Back East they had to buy it. When we left that land all the way from $25 to $75 an acre, because here it was produced just about as good as that was. Only you didn't have the market here that we had there. But then you had to send out the land and you could crop it. But we didn't have nothing when we came out here. We had to just take a brush, clean up our land.

speaker: William Kauder
words: We did go down, kind of up the river from Pasco. But we looked at 160 there, the level of the land. But it was, I don't know how many thousand feet above water. A nice level flat land.

speaker: William Kauder
words: But it was too dry. That was the trouble here then.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was this a pretty wild country when you arrived? What do you mean, wild animals?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh wild animals and a hard country, a harsh country.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it a hard life?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, it wasn't bad.

speaker: William Kauder
words: You go through the woods, you might travel all day and never see anything. All at once maybe you'll run up against the bear, something like that.

speaker: William Kauder
words: But then the bear was wild and he'd give you plenty of room.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How about the people? How did the people enforce the law in those days? Before there were marshals and sheriffs?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well I don't know about that.

speaker: William Kauder
words: We had officers just about the same as they got now. But without only we didn't have them,

speaker: William Kauder
words: what you might call them, on a mobile.

speaker: William Kauder
words: We met quite a lot of Indians, they were civil and peaceable.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were the Indians still moving pretty freely through this part of the country?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, not here. But generally it remains on the reservations.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What town would you go to when you wanted to get supplies?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How was that? What town did you go to when you wanted to get supplies?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You mean for groceries in the church, sir?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes. Kendrick.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What happened in those days?

speaker: William Kauder
words: ... They had several dry goods stores, grocery stores, saloons. Hotels. Yeah.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Hotels. You go to the hotel and you get a good meal for two bits.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Beefsteak.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: About how often would you go to town from the homestead?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, not very often. In the fall of the year we tried to get our supplies so we didn't have to go in the winter time. Because the chill where we lived would get about four feet deep and down the country guard there was bare ground. So one drink wouldn't work.

speaker: William Kauder
words: That's the reason we would take in the harvest, you know. Get enough money and get us a good supply of something. Unless the fella says we'd hole up in the winter. Unless you know it got so awful deep.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well how would people keep themselves entertained then?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, we had a school and I believe there was about 20 -25 kids going. And every Friday night we had a literary.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And on Sunday, we had Sunday school and church. We could get a preacher but we always had Sunday school.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was a literary?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, it was kind of a debate in society. We'd get a subject and then debate on it. Then we had a program, you know. Declinations.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would everybody participate?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Some of them old writers are pretty good talkers on a subject.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well sometimes I have a kind of a funny question up for debate. My question is more essential.

speaker: William Kauder
words: The dish rag or the broom?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yeah, we used to have lots of fun at the meetings.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How about parties and such? Were there many of those?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, once in a while they had a dance. Not in our neighborhood but across from the Gold Hill country they'd have dances there. We'd have a kind of what you call a play party, you know. No dancing allowed.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why wasn't there any dancing allowed?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, there was kind of religious..., you know, and they didn't believe in it. Then they'd have a party at those places.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think many of the early homesteaders cleared out around that 1893 time?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Done what? Do you think many of the homesteaders cleared out in that 1893 hard times?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did many of them kind of go broke and have to leave?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know whether you'd call it go broke or not but lots of them about that time would have served their five years and they'd go and prove up and then mortgage it and leave it. There was lots of land in there now still in the hands of companies. Yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would they sell out to the timber company or what? Do what? Did they sell out to the timber company usually?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No. J .P. Wahlberg bought most of their land.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He was the banker up in Troy, wasn't he?

speaker: William Kauder
words: At Lawson. He was a good banker.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was he a pretty good banker?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, he was a good banker. He looked out.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did he kind of watch out for people and try to help them out?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No.

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, he wasn't about to come to a man.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know how he worked but he'd take a mortgage on their property and of course foreclose it. He'd put that in so much deposit in the bank. It was a backing for the bank.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you remember the Kendrick fire in 1903?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that here then?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes. We lived up here on the C -group. I went down there after it all cooled down to a pretty shabby looking place.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were the businesses still trying to operate?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes. They put up kinds of temporary buildings and started over.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did the politicians campaign for office around that time?

speaker: William Kauder
words: There was quite a number running for office.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Of course, I go all the time. Some are successful and some aren't.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How would they come around and let people know who they were or who was running? Well, you see, you take now, I don't know that in Moscow that man Snow, what is he running for Congress? Now you mean? Now you mean?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I'm not sure. I think he might be. I'm talking about back in the early days.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would the politicians who were running come out to the different little communities?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, that's just what I was going to tell you.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, they had their cards, you know. They'd come and maybe they'd announce that they were speaking at certain places at certain nights. Sometimes we'd meet there with them. Sometimes it would be all dozens of Democrats or a dozen Republicans. There'd be different times, you know. Now they come there and from Moscow they would come out and they'd talk. They'd have them meeting there at the schoolhouse and we'd go over there and hear them. There'd be maybe half a dozen or a dozen Republicans or maybe next time that many Democrats would come. Each one had their story.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were most of the early homesteaders pretty interested in politics?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, they weren't crazy about it. They didn't care too much.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of things were they crazy about?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, I don't know what they were.

speaker: William Kauder
words: They were very badly on anything or any subject.

speaker: William Kauder
words: They had the work. Some of them made shakes. Some of them made shingles. They were seen to make a dollar, you know, to buy a grub. They were busy.

speaker: William Kauder
words: My father and I made shingles one whole winter. We made a better kind of building and we had a shed. We'd drag a log in there and we'd sew it up in the shingle. We'd lance 16 inches and then we'd split them up and drive them and shave them. Put them up in the pack. We'd get maybe two dollars a thousand for them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, how'd you do something?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When people went on down into Kendrick for their supplies, would they pretty often go on a bender or something to get away from the pressures of working so hard?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Never heard of it.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I heard at one time in the early days at the Fourth of July celebrations in Kendrick, they used to have big tug -o -wars and the people from Potlatch Ridge would take on everybody else.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you remember those? You mean they had kind of a tug -o -war?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: The Potlatch had quite a bunch of big heavy men, way over 200. And they got all one end of the rope.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, several from Leland, men that weighed 220 and 225 pounds, you know. There was a pot of those in the then. And the Welkirches, some of the Welkirches were big heavy men.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What other kinds of things would happen at those Fourth of July celebrations?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, I don't know. Down at Kendrick they had on the Fourth of July, they never had races of all kinds. They had a large platform outside, you know, I don't know what you call it, a pavilion or something. They had a dance there and they would start that dance after dinner about three o 'clock, two or three. And they danced I guess maybe all night, I don't know. I lived there about eleven, twelve o 'clock and they were still a -hammering away.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where was it that you were born again? In Hot State?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Illinois.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When was that?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did your father decide to leave Illinois?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, the land at that time was quite high in Illinois and we got just as good a land, a whole lot cheaper, across the Mississippi River in Missouri. Oh, but the land was just as good as the Illinois land and didn't have to pay half as much. They had to take land in Illinois, they were just selling for fifteen, seventy -five dollars a acre to go and buy just as good a land for fifteen and twenty dollars in Cook County, Missouri.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were there still a lot of hard feelings around that time about the Civil War?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, not right in there, but south where there was. It wasn't very healthy to take a family from the north to go into our Kansas or Mississippi and places and locate.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where did you go from Missouri?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Iowa.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did you stay there?

speaker: William Kauder
words: One year.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Broke up.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your father homestead in Iowa too?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Uh -uh.

speaker: William Kauder
words: There was no homestead land.

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, Iowa, yeah.

speaker: William Kauder
words: A lot of awful changes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What do you think is the most impressive sort of change that you've seen?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, the biggest change is in the way things are running. The worst change I think now is Watergate business.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And that's, uh,

speaker: William Kauder
words: this, uh, gasoline business.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Things mix up pretty bad.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How do you think Watergate compares to something like Teapot Dome?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't think it's much different, is there.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know. I wasn't around when Teapot Dome happened.

speaker: William Kauder
words: How old are you if I'm allowed to ask?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, if you stay alive you're going to see lots of changes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did your family come west? How did they travel?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, we come on the train.

speaker: William Kauder
words: We bought our tickets in Sioux City, Iowa. And we went from Sioux City to Omaha, Nebraska and we got on the Union Pacific and we come on out to Walla Walla.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That was about 1891?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, that was in 1890.

speaker: William Kauder
words: 1891 we come up in this country.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I asked you before about Coxey's Army and you didn't say much about it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I wonder, were there many people who got interested in that Coxey's Army thing around here?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No. No, they didn't take no stock in it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of a thing was that anyway?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, it was a lot of people who were unsatisfied with the way the government was running things. They were really just no work for them, you know. That's what it was after, was the war on wages.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What were they going to do about it?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, they wanted to talk to the President of the United States to start something. But I don't know what they wanted to start so they could have employment and make a living.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there much business with the IWWs in this area?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, not around here.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of jobs did you do throughout your life?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, worked the righteous and saw mills.

speaker: William Kauder
words: When we first come west to Leonard and Walla Walla, I think it was the 17th of March when we landed in Walla Walla. They had all their crops in. So we moved from Walla Walla on up to Dayton and Father and I got a job in the saw mill there. We worked that summer and that winter in the saw mill. It was made enough to get us a team wagon harness and kitchen supply of stuff and we hauled that up here.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you know where you were headed for when you left Dayton? And you came all the way by wagon?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yep.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know, it don't take very long to go from here down to Dayton now, but it took us about eight days to move up from Dayton to what you call Crescent. See you real quick.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When you arrived at your homestead it was just unproved land, wasn't it? It's what? When you arrived at your homestead it was just empty land, wasn't it? What did you do first when you got there?

speaker: William Kauder
words: There was a cabin to live in.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did that take?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I was out in the harvest that fall and Father he cut and hewed the logs. And when I come back from harvest I took the team and drug them up and we had what you call a house raving. And we put up the frame mark that fall and then it snowed us under and we couldn't work anymore until the next spring.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where did you live that winter then?

speaker: William Kauder
words: There on the homestead we put up a box house. I remember it was sixteen. I think sixteen feet square.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I had a fireplace on one end of it and a cook stove and a couple of beds in there and it fared pretty good.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How many people came over for your house raving?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, we had a dozen or more just waters in the neighborhood, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did it take?

speaker: William Kauder
words: The best part of one day.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there a celebration or a big feed or something afterwards?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yeah, we had a dinner.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was helping out like that pretty common in those homesteading days?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What other ways would people help each other out?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, sometimes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Thickness or something like that because there's always willing to help.

speaker: William Kauder
words: We had a doctor at Gleeelen and we had another doctor who lived on a homestead there at Gold Hill. So, other...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were you and your father supposed to come and help other people out who helped you out raving your house?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, always.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kinds of things did you do to help pay back?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well,

speaker: William Kauder
words: some had a barn to raise. I put up a log barn.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I'd help all that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were there any people who weren't very neighborly, who didn't participate in those sorts of things?

speaker: William Kauder
words: People you might call sort of hermits or...

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, yes. Yes, they all...

speaker: William Kauder
words: When you had a gathering of those kinds, everybody was happy, well pleased.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Is it clear outside or...?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: No, it's cloudy today. There was probably about an inch or two of snow this morning. It's not snowing now, but it's cloudy out.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think the weather has changed much from the early days?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: We don't get near the snow that we used to.

speaker: William Kauder
words: It's more rain in the weather time than we did then.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you have any idea why that is?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No. I don't.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kinds of words would you use to describe the old days in general?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How was that? How would you describe the old days? The homestead days?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well... There was difference, lots of difference. Some people... had had an income or didn't have to get out and stretch. They had to make it pretty good on the homestead. But if you couldn't make it on the homestead and had to work out for a living, it was pretty tough going.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Whenever you worked out, you were losing not much on the homestead. Clearing.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: About how much of a year did you have to work out?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I generally went out and harvested much about all.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think people were happier in those days?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I don't know about that.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Some of them were quite well contested and some were not. Just same as they are now. I don't see much difference. I don't see much difference.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there much difference between the real homesteading years and 20 or 30 years later?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: They had more land and cultivation and there was...

speaker: William Kauder
words: a whole lot further advanced. They had more stock, something to sell, you know. I would have cattle and maybe some hogs. I would have something to sell. Hard labor in the grain or anything, not from these timber ranches. They generally fell in. And the roads wasn't much more than a common trail. You couldn't haul much of the load over them. So to feed it and drive over it like driving several heads of stock is just better than hauling a load of grain over a trail.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And bumping over them darn rocks in the road.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That was when you were coming out with horses, wasn't it?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you prefer working with horses?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, yes. I never learned to drive a car.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How about mules? Did you ever work with mules?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Back East, my father had a set of mules. It was about as good a team as I ever worked.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I was 15, 14, 15 years old.

speaker: William Kauder
words: We raised a lot of corn. Corn was the principal crop. We put in plow on about the first of March for oats. And we sold maybe 20 or 30 acres in oats. And then we plowed for corn. Put in about 30 or 40 acres in corn. We'd do it all with then two mules.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And a 12 inch plow. And a double A harrow.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think a span of mules could outwork a span of horses?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, I don't think so. What I liked about the mules was they were steady. They just lug along, you know. All day. Horses, they'd be a little fast in the morning, and they'd be slower in the evening. Mules not. The mules had that gait, and that would back it all day.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why didn't you use mules out here then?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know why. Because there was a man moved in over there to Gold Hill. He'd come from the southern part of Missouri, pretty close to the Oklahoma line. And he brought his span of mules with him out here. And he kept them three or four years. I don't know whether he sold them or traded them for horses, but he got rid of them. I don't know why.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Are mules as stubborn as they say they are?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, the span we had wasn't the little tougher in the mouth. You had to pull a little harder to guide them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: About how many years could you work a horse or a mule before it would give out on you?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, I don't know. I never tried that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you ever have any horses that were real favorites of yours? What? Did you ever have any horses that were real favorites of yours?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You've been an animal I like better than others?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, I had one Gilligan. He was a chestnut sorrel. He weighed about 1 ,200. A heavy, blockish animal. And I don't know if I could do anything with him. Plow him single, double, hitch him up to anything. He worked with that. An aragon, he was the last animal.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was his name?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Selim.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How many years did you have him?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't just remember. I guess about 10 or 12 years.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Could you work him even when he was getting to be an old horse? Did I work him with other horses? Did you work him even when he was getting to be 10 or 12 years old?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, he was alright then, yes.
